summary: Simon advocates for tech independence through self-hosting and owning your digital infrastructure, arguing that buying your own domain and hosting your own services compounds over time. He shares his personal journey from vim/Neovim to building a homelab, emphasizing that while self-hosting requires effort, the joy comes from learning, using what you build, and not depending on companies that can kill products or raise prices. The post celebrates open-source software and the community that shares code freely, highlighting tools like Listmonk, GoatCounter, and Quartz that power his online presence. His core message: start with a domain, learn Linux fundamentals, and gradually build independence from big tech platforms.
key_points: Owning your domain and self-hosting compounds over time - watching people migrate from WordPress to Medium to Substack losing backlinks and history is painful
Tech independence means not depending on any particular company or software, learned from Derek Sivers
The initial learning curve for self-hosting seems hard but becomes obvious once you overcome the first hurdles
Open-source creates a beautiful community where millions give away work for free, enabling others to build on top
Self-hosting provides independence from companies like Google that routinely kill beloved products or raise prices
money_quotes: If people ask me how they should start writing or how to get a job, I always say to buy a domain first.
Every time they had a new domain. To me, that's so sad.
Initially everything seems hard, but once you know how, it's kind of obvious and less hard.
Tech Independence basically means that you do not depend on any particular company or software.
We always believe we just have to use what's out there to buy, but there are other ways.
Open-source and open-source code is beautiful. It's much more than just using someone else's software, but it's all the millions of people who just give away their work for free.
Linux runs the world. There is almost no digital device that we use that is not running Linux or part of it.
You also don't get a heart attack if Google turns off your favorite app.
key_insight: Self-hosting and tech independence isn't just about avoiding subscription costs—it's about the compounding value of ownership, the joy of building and learning, and freedom from platform dependency.
